data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-07T01:37:24.819Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating .NET Framework code that uses Thread.Abort to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort at all. It uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work fine in modern .NET.

Let me invoke the thread-abort-migration skill since it's available and relevant to the user's question about Thread.Abort migration.

Wait, actually, looking at the skill description: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


So this code doesn't actually need the skill. The code doesn't use Thread.Abort, Thread.Interrupt, ThreadAbortException, or Thread.ResetAbort—it only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep which work identically in modern .NET. I should tell the user that their code doesn't actually use Thread.Abort and doesn't need migration for that specific concern, though I could suggest modernizing to use Task/async patterns as a general improvement.
